_source: Sourced from the Atlas Reasoning Engine writeup and Salesforce's Agentforce product documentation. Salesforce's tool concept is 'Actions' bound to 'Topics' — Topics are the configuration unit that determines which Actions an agent can invoke. Specific Action schemas are accessible only to Salesforce customers via Trailhead and the Agentforce admin console; this file documents the patterns Salesforce publicly describes.

topic.description: A predefined intent the agent can handle (e.g., 'process_refund', 'schedule_appointment', 'lookup_order'). Each Topic carries: natural-language instructions, business policies, and an allowed set of Actions.
topic.implication: Topics are first-class GUARDRAILS — an agent cannot invoke an Action that isn't in the current Topic's allowed set. The universe of possible behavior is bounded by Topic configuration, not discovered at runtime.
topic.configured_by: Salesforce admins / partners via the Agentforce Studio admin console (not by the LLM)

action.description: The agent's tool primitive. Five major action types are supported.

_topics_example.instructions: Help customers who want to return an item and receive a refund. Verify order eligibility against policy before processing.
policies: Refunds outside the 30-day window require supervisor authorization
Fraud-flagged accounts must escalate to fraud team

_topics_example.instructions: Help customers schedule, reschedule, or cancel appointments with sales reps.
policies: Only schedule within rep availability windows

_action_example.name: process_refund_under_threshold
_action_example.description: Issue a refund up to $200 against a customer order.
_action_example._topic_binding: process_refund (this Action is invokable ONLY within this Topic)

validation_layer.strategy_1_topic_scoping: The Topic configuration restricts which Actions the agent can invoke. An agent assigned to topic_id='schedule_appointment' literally cannot select 'process_refund_under_threshold' — it's not in the topic's allowed_actions.
validation_layer.strategy_2_einstein_trust_layer: Output of each Action's input/output passes through the Einstein Trust Layer: PII masking, toxicity check, prompt-injection detection, hallucination mitigation.
validation_layer.strategy_3_iterative_refinement: Atlas 'evaluates LLM-generated plans through self-reflection or built-in verification, fine-tuning solutions until valid outputs emerge' (per Atlas technical writeup).
what_this_protects_against: PII leakage in Action arguments or outputs
what_it_does_NOT_protect_against_nativel: Semantically-wrong-but-policy-allowed argument values (developer-implemented Apex / Flow logic)
Foreign-key existence (typically handled by Salesforce data model)
Business-rule violations (developer encodes in Topic policies or Apex)

dry_run_mode.mechanism: Salesforce admins typically configure Agentforce in a sandbox org with simulated data before promoting to production. Built-in 'preview' for many Actions, but per-run dry-run for arbitrary execution is sandbox-based, not in-product.
dry_run_mode.comparison_to_anthropic_plan_mode: Salesforce's dry-run is environment-based (sandbox org), while Anthropic's permission_mode='plan' is per-run. Different shape.

approval_gate.mechanism: Topic policies + 'transfer to human' fallback actions. The Atlas writeup states: 'The system includes automatic transfer to human fallback actions when confidence drops or policy violations threaten.'
human_review_options: Live agent handoff (Service Cloud agent receives full context)
Action-level guards (Topic policy: 'this Action requires supervisor authorization')

_buyer_followups: Request a Topic configuration example for our specific use case — what's the Topic shape, what Actions are bound, what policies are encoded?
What's the cost / time to add a new Action to an existing Topic? (Often nontrivial — typically Salesforce partner / professional services work)
How granular is the Einstein Trust Layer audit? Can we get per-Action policy decisions in our SIEM?
MCP integration — what auth model does it use? Can we connect external tools without exposing internal credentials to Salesforce-managed infrastructure?
